DESIGN JUSTICE RESOURCES • The Design Justice Network offers resources and conducts events for those practicing the design justice principles. See: https://designjustice.org • Design Justice adopts an activist approach to design, focusing on traditionally underserved communities. It challenges the extent to which universal principles are valid in understanding the diverse needs of users. Sasha Costanza-Chock’s book provides a comprehensive review of theories and issues in design. – Costanza-Chock, Sasha. Design Justice: Community-Led Practices to Build the Worlds We Need. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press, 2020. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/12255.001.0001

The phrase “design thinking” grew out of a merger between two design firms, David Kelly Design and ID Two (see in Brown and Wyatt, 2010, page 33). The design processes these firms were using were quite different from standard product design, focusing primarily on designing experiences for consumers in addition to products themselves. Brown, Tim and Jocelyn Wyatt. “Design Thinking for Social Innovation.” Development Outreach 12, no. 1 (2010): 29–43. https://doi.org/10.1596/1020-797X_12_1_29 Knemeyer (2015) maintains that user experience and design thinking have shared roots, though the concept of UX precedes design thinking by a decade. Knemeyer even calls them “two sides of the same coin” (page 66).
